name: Dolan v. United States Postal Service
summary: Held that the postal exception covers bad things that happen to the mail itself (not arriving, arriving damaged or late) but does not extend to separate injuries like slip-and-falls that merely occur around mail delivery.
significance: The central precedent interpreting the postal exception, which held the exception does not bar claims for injuries caused by how mail is placed (slip-and-fall from a package left on steps) and articulated a general rule about what injuries fall within the exception.
